sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 45, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 44 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the right chamber's structure more clearly:
- Rows 9-13: All black (BBBBB...), no opening
- Rows 14-18: YYOO (opening) then black
- Rows 19-20: YYOOOOOOO (wider opening) then black
- Rows 39-43: YYOOOOOOOOOOOO (very wide opening) then black
- Rows 44-48: YYOOSSSSS (square here) then black
- Rows 49-50: YYOO then black
So the right chamber is the black region on the right side. The square at (44,34) occupies cols 34-38. The black region on the right starts at col 34 and extends to col 50.
Looking at the structure, the right chamber has a "narrow" top (rows 9-13) and a "wide" bottom (rows 39-50). The square can move up to row 39 at col 39, but not higher because the chamber narrows.
Wait, let me re-examine. At rows 9-13, the right chamber is all black (cols 34-50). So the square COULD potentially move to row 9 at col 34, if there's a path. But the chamber narrows at rows 19-38, so the square can't pass through.
Actually, looking at rows 19-20: YYOOOOOOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBO
The opening is cols 30-38 (YYOOOOOOO = 9 cells), then black starts at col 39.
So at rows 19-20, the right chamber is cols 39-50. The square at col 34 would be in the opening, not in the black shape.
This means the right chamber has different widths at different rows:
- Rows 9-13: cols 34-50 (17 cells wide)
- Rows 14-18: cols 34-50 (but with an opening at cols 30-33)
- Rows 19-38: cols 39-50 (12 cells wide)
- Rows 39-50: cols 34-50 (17 cells wide)
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-read the ASCII.
Row 14: YYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBO
Cols 30-31: YY, cols 32-33: OO, cols 34-50: BBBB...B, col 51: O
So the black shape on the right starts at col 34 and extends to col 50.
Row 19: YYOOOOOOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBO
Cols 30-38: YYOOOOOOO (9 cells), cols 39-50: BBBB...B, col 51: O
So at row 19, the black shape on the right starts at col 39 and extends to col 50.
This means the right chamber narrows at row 19: the left boundary moves from col 34 to col 39.
So the right chamber has:
- Rows 9-18: left boundary at col 34
- Rows 19-38: left boundary at col 39
- Rows 39-50: left boundary at col 34
The square is 5 cells wide. At rows 9-18, it can be at cols 34, 39, 44 (since the chamber is 17 cells wide, cols 34-50).
At rows 19-38, it can be at cols 39, 44 (since the chamber is 12 cells wide, cols 39-50).
At rows 39-50, it can be at cols 34, 39, 44.
So the square can move from rows 39-50 (col 34) to rows 9-18 (col 34) by going through rows 19-38... but wait, at rows 19-38, the chamber starts at col 39, not col 34. So the square at col 34 can't pass through rows 19-38.
This means the right chamber is divided into two disconnected regions:
- Top: rows 9-18, cols 34-50
- Bottom: rows 39-50, cols 34-50
- Middle: rows 19-38, cols 39-50
The square can move between the top and middle regions (cols 34-50 at top, cols 39-50 at middle), but it needs to be at col 39 or higher to pass through the middle.
So to get the square from the bottom (rows 39-50, col 34) to the top (rows 9-18, col 34), it needs to:
1. Move RIGHT to col 39 (from col 34)
2. Move UP through the middle (rows 19-38, col 39)
3. Move LEFT to col 34 (from col 39) once in the top region
Let me try this!
Current: left at (9,24), right at (44,34)
1. RIGHT: right square moves to (44,39)
2. UP: right square moves to (39,39)
3. UP: right square moves to (34,39)
4. UP: right square moves to (29,39)
5. UP: right square moves to (24,39)
6. UP: right square moves to (19,39)
7. UP: right square moves to (14,39)
8. UP: right square moves to (9,39)
9. LEFT: right square moves to (9,34)
But wait, I need to check if the square can actually move through these positions. Let me first try RIGHT to move the right square to col 39.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left at (9,24), right at (44,34)
# Try to move right square to col 39, then UP through the middle
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
positions = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        bid = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in bid]
        cols = [p[1] for p in bid]
        positions.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
print(f"After RIGHT: {sorted(positions)}")

# Now try UP multiple times
for i in range(6):
    result = action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    positions = []
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S':
            bid = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in bid]
            cols = [p[1] for p in bid]
            positions.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
    print(f"After UP {i+1}: {sorted(positions)}")
    if last_action_result.get('level_completed'):
        print("LEVEL COMPLETED!")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
